title: How to Think About Solar and Inverter Sizing Before You Buy
summary: A decision guide focused on load planning, battery expectations, and how backup hours shape the entire system budget.
seoDescription: A beginner-friendly guide to solar and inverter sizing, backup planning, and realistic battery expectations.
imageAlt: Technician wiring a solar inverter setup with safety equipment
points: List critical loads first instead of trying to power every appliance at once.
Battery sizing should be tied to realistic backup hours, not guesswork.
Inverter size should reflect startup demand, not just steady-state use.

sections.heading: Start with critical loads
body: The fastest way to overspend on solar is to size for every appliance instead of the loads that matter most during an outage.
A critical-load list usually includes lighting, refrigeration, networking, and a few key sockets. That list creates a much better starting point than a vague request for whole-house backup.

sections.heading: Battery planning changes the budget quickly
body: Most clients focus on panel count first, but backup duration is often what drives the more expensive decisions in the system.
Once you know the number of hours you expect from the battery bank, sizing conversations become more grounded and the quote becomes more realistic.

sections.heading: Inverter size is not just a watts conversation
body: Some appliances have startup demands that exceed their normal running load, which is why inverter selection should not be based on a rough estimate alone.
A good installer will ask about the actual devices you plan to power, not just the total you guessed from memory.
